---
title: "Female Powerlifting: Performing Kruskal-Wallis and Dunn's Test Analyses Across Age Groups"
author:
  - name: "Keelin Joyce"
    email: kjoyc22@stlawu.edu
    affiliation:
      - id: slu
        name: St. Lawrence University
  - name: "Ivan Ramler"
    email: iramler@stlawu.edu
    affiliation:
      - ref: slu

date: March 25, 2026
description: This module explores results from powerlifting competitions, comparing ranks between age groups.
categories: 
  - non-parametric analysis
  - Kruskal-Wallis Test
  - post-hoc Dunn's Test
image: "https://www.greatestphysiques.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Stefanie-Cohen.05.jpg"
---

![Female Powerlifter: Stefanie Cohen](https://www.greatestphysiques.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/10/Stefanie-Cohen.05.jpg){width="75%"}

## Welcome Video

For those interested in how some events in powerlifting competitions look, please check out this video from Day 1 of Powerlifting America Open Nationals from @A7Intl.

<iframe width="360" height="640" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/x3wKUPmtSEs" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" allowfullscreen>

</iframe>

## Introduction

In powerlifting competitions, athletes compete in three compound events to lift as much weight as possible. [Bench press](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/hWbUlkb5Ms4?feature=share){target="_blank"} is done with athletes laying flat on their back and lowering the bar to their chest and lifting back up, targeting upper body (specifically chest, triceps, and shoulders). [Deadlifts](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/ZaTM37cfiDs?feature=share){target="_blank"} are a full body lift in which athletes lift a bar from the ground to hip level (targets glutes, hamstrings, and back). [Squats](https://www.youtube.com/shorts/PPmvh7gBTi0){target="_blank"} are done with a barbell on their back, just below the shoulders, and hinging until the hips are lower than the top of the knee (targeting quads, glutes, hamstrings, and core).

This module analyzes results from powerlifting competitions. Results are found at [OpenPowerlifting](https://www.openpowerlifting.org){target="_blank"}, a project aimed at collecting powerlifting data over time. Performances are ranked by how much weight is lifted and athletes are sorted into age classes. You will compare the rankings achieved by athletes across age classes and distinguish any differences between classes.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Activity Length" appearance="minimal"}
This activity would likely be suitable for an in-class example that lasts roughly 60 minutes as well as extensions that can be assigned as work outside of class or a second class period.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Learning Objectives" appearance="minimal"}
For this activity, students will primarily use their previous knowledge of ANOVA and Tukey testing to apply it to non parametric data.

By the end of the activity, you will be able to:

1.  Assess and sort large data sets appropriately in order to run statistical tests.
2.  Analyze non-parametric comparisons using the Kruskal-Wallis Test.
3.  Assess differences between two groups using Dunn's Test for multiple comparison.
4.  Construct a visualization to display results from testing.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Methods" appearance="minimal"}
In this module, students will use Open Powerlifting data to assess that comparisons in performance between age classes.

They will use the Kruskal-Wallis Test for comparison after organizing the data appropriately for the test to run.

After finding a discernible difference between groups, students will run the Dunn's Test of pairwise comparison and create an appropriate visualization to show which groups have discernible medians.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Technology Requirements" appearance="minimal"}
The testing is designed to be completed using RStudio and is not designed to be done by hand.

This module assumes basic familiarity with the use of RStudio, but sample code is provided so proficiency is not required.

Analyses can be done on paper following the testing completed in RStudio.
:::

## Data

The initial data set from [OpenPowerlifting Dataset](https://www.openpowerlifting.org){target="_blank"} contains over 3,000,000 rows and 42 columns. We will be using a separate female and male data set taken from the larger set, each containing 15 variables and 300 individual observations. These have been condensed for this module from a female data set and male data set from [SCORE Sports Data Repository](https://data.scorenetwork.org/powerlifting/openpowerlifting.html){target="_blank"}. Each row represents a powerlifting athlete and variables including their demographics and how much weight they lifted (in kilograms).

For the convenience of this module, the data has been further cleaned and can be downloaded here. Download wrangled and cleaned data:

**Male athletes**: [openpl_male.csv](openpl_male.csv)

**Female athletes**: [openpl_female.csv](openpl_female.csv)

#### Data Source

Adapted from [OpenPowerlifting](https://www.openpowerlifting.org0){target="_blank"} and [SCORE Sports Data Repository](https://data.scorenetwork.org/powerlifting/openpowerlifting.html){target="_blank"}

<details>

<summary><b>Descriptions of Relevant Variables</b></summary>

| Variable | Description |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Name | Name of the athlete |
| Age | Age of the athlete on the start date |
| AgeClass | Age class the athlete falls in (e.g. 40-45) |
| BodyweightKg | Recorded bodyweight of athlete in kilograms at the start of the event |
| Squat | Weight in kilograms of the athlete's best attempt at squatting |
| Bench | Weight in kilograms of the athlete's best attempt at benching |
| Deadlift | Weight in kilograms of the athlete's best attempt at deadlifting |
| TotalKg | Sum of weight lifted between the three exercises |

## Performing the Tests

#### Load in the Data

```{r warning=FALSE, output=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)

## download data from above before loading

openpl_male <- read_csv("~/Desktop/SYE/github repos/stat450-spr2026-score/keelinjoyce/powerlifting//openpl_male.csv")

openpl_female <- read_csv("~/Desktop/SYE/github repos/stat450-spr2026-score/keelinjoyce/powerlifting//openpl_female.csv")
```

## Kruskal-Wallis Test of Differences

The Kruskal-Wallis test is a non-parametric test for centrality to determine if there are statistical differences between independent groups. It is typically used as an alternative to ANOVA testing on non-normal data and compares the medians of the data as opposed to the means.

ANOVA testing is used to compare the means if groups to test for statistical discernibility. This only works for normally distributed data. When looking at data that is skewed, like what we have here, we need to use a different test. Because there are a few elite performers that skew the data, it is more appropriate to look at the medians of age classes instead of the means.

The Kruskal-Wallis test is used with population medians as opposed to means when the data is continuous or ordinal (as powerlifting data would be). It is typically used in place of ANOVA testing when the normality assumption is violated. The test requires the assumptions of independence and a large enough sample size (n\>5) to still hold.

$H = \frac{12}{N(N+1)} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{R_i^2}{n_i} - 3(N+1)$

where

*N: total number of observations*

*k: number of groups*

$R_i$*: sum of ranks in i-th group*

$n_i$*: number of observations in i-th group*

#### General steps to run Kruskal-Wallis test

Form a hypothesis where the structure is similar to an ANOVA hypotheses.

    -   null hypothesis is population medians are equal

    -   alternate hypothesis is that at least one group is different

Rank data from smallest to largest from all groups.

Find the sum of the ranks for each group.

Use test in R to calculate the Kruskal-Wallis H statistic.

If the p-value is less than 0.05, reject the null indicating as least one group differs slightly.

If the null is rejected, use Dunn's test (multiple comparison test) to find which groups specifically differ.

#### Forming a Hypothesis

For a Kruskal-Wallis test,similar to an ANOVA test, the null hypothesis is always that there is no difference in medians between groups and the alternative hypothesis claims that there is a difference between at least two groups.

$H_0:$ there is no difference in the median of powerlifting weight between age groups

$H_a:$ at least one group differs from another

To run the Kruskal-Wallis test, the data gets ranked from 1-n before being grouped by level, in this case `AgeClass`.

We want to rank the data before it is grouped so the overall rankings and where they land can be compared between groups.

Arrange the data appropriately and rank it before grouping by `AgeClass`:

```{r echo=TRUE, output = FALSE}
female_bench_ranked <- openpl_female |> arrange(desc(Bench)) |>
  mutate(rank = row_number()) |>
  filter(!is.na(AgeClass)) |>
  mutate(AgeClass = fct_relevel(AgeClass, "5-12")) |>
  select(Name, Age, AgeClass, Bench, rank)|>
  mutate(AgeClass = fct_recode(AgeClass,
                              "05-12" = "5-12"))
## recoding "5-12" as "05-12" so it appears in the correct order

female_bench_ranked |>
  group_by(AgeClass)  |>
  summarise(n())
```

Create a boxplot to look at the distribution of bench weight across age classes:

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE}
ggplot(data = female_bench_ranked, aes(x = AgeClass, y = Bench)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()
```

The boxplot shows the general trend of the data increases to a peak between 20-34 and steadily decreases after that.

Run the test using the `kruskal.test()` function:

Using the standard alpha value of 0.05, if the p-value is larger than that there is no reason to reject the null hypothesis but any smaller value would indicate a difference in medians between AgeClass and we would have reason to reject the null.

```{r}
female_bench_results <- kruskal.test(female_bench_ranked$rank ~ 
                                       female_bench_ranked$AgeClass)
female_bench_results
```

![The test output reveals the p-value < 2.2e-16 ](~/Desktop/SYE/github repos/stat450-spr2026-score/keelinjoyce/powerlifting/testresults.png)

Using an alpha value of 0.5, the p-value is much smaller indicating that our results are statistically discernible, or significant, and we can claim that there is a statistical difference between at least two groups.

Because the Kruskal-Wallis test only determines *if* there is a discernible difference between groups, we need to use a post-hoc test to determine *which* groups are statistically discernible from each other. The most appropriate post-hoc test following a Kruskal-Wallis test is the Dunn's Test.

### Dunn's Test of Multiple Comparisons

The Dunn's Test is a pairwise comparison test (similar to a Tukey Test) that determines which groups are discernibly different from each other once we have determined there are differences using a predetermined alpha level (here we will use the standard $\alpha = 0.05$). 

Load in the necessary packages for the test:

```{r warning=FALSE, output=FALSE}
library(dunn.test)
library(FSA)
library(reshape2)
```

Use the `dunn.test()` function to compile p-values of the differences between groups and form a table of the values:

```{r output=FALSE}
female_bench_dunn <- dunn.test(female_bench_ranked$rank, female_bench_ranked$AgeClass, method = "bh")
dunn_results_df <- as.data.frame(female_bench_dunn$P.adjusted, female_bench_dunn$comparisons) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("comparison") |>
  separate(comparison, into = c("group1", "group2"), sep = " - ") |>
  rename("p_value" = `female_bench_dunn$P.adjusted`)
```

## Visualizing Results

Getting an idea of which p-values indicate a statistical discernibility is difficult when there are so many. Creating a visualization of the values is the easiest way to see which groups are different and which are not.

Create a heatmap of the p-values to see where the results are discernible:

```{r}
female_bench_dunn <- dunn.test(female_bench_ranked$rank, female_bench_ranked$AgeClass, method = "bh")
dunn_results_df <- as.data.frame(female_bench_dunn$P.adjusted, female_bench_dunn$comparisons) |>
  tibble::rownames_to_column("comparison") |>
  separate(comparison, into = c("group1", "group2"), sep = " - ") |>
  rename("p_value" = `female_bench_dunn$P.adjusted`)

dunn_results_df$log_p <- -log10(dunn_results_df$p_value)

ggplot(dunn_results_df, aes(x = group1, y = group2, fill = p_value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "forestgreen", high = "grey100") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The -log10 p-value heatmap shows us how many zeros are following the decimal point indicating that the blue values are more statistically different. It is difficult to conceptualize the differences of these p-values with this visual.

Using an alpha value of 0.05, construct another heatmap indicating which values are discernible or not.

```{r}
dunn_results_df$sig <- if_else(dunn_results_df$p_value <= 0.05,1,0)

ggplot(dunn_results_df, aes(x = group1, y = group2, fill = sig)) +
  geom_tile(color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_continuous(low = "grey100", high = "forestgreen") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The results in light blue show there is not a statistical discernibility between the two groups. The dark blue groups are recognized as different from each other.

If the pairwise test was completed using a different alpha value, the heatmap would look different. Try using different alpha values (e.g. $\alpha = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.01$) and look at how the results differ.

::: {.callout-important collapse="true" title="Additional Exercises and Extensions"}
This module can be extended using the datasets provided for further variable testing.

-   This module is done using female benching data, but provided is also data on female squatting, female deadlifting, and all three exercises for males
-   The module can be extended further to examine differences between exercises and genders in addition to age groups.
-   A sample module for female squatting can be found here ***add sample here***
:::

## Conclusion
